title: Planning
noActivePlan: You don't have an active plan yet.

step1.title: What is your goal?
step1.targetTimeHint: Leave empty if you have no time goal.
step1.eventDateHint: Plan duration will be calculated automatically.
step1.eventDateHintWeeks: Recommended: {min}–{max} weeks depending on your level. Duration will be adjusted to your date.

step3.title: Confirm your level
step3.subtitle: Adjust if necessary. These zones will guide your plan generation.
step3.adjustHint: Adjust with the slider (±5 around the estimate)
step3.reassurance: Your plan will automatically adapt to this level. You can always adjust it later.

step4.title: Plan parameters
step4.planDurationWarning: Warning: less than 8 weeks is too short for an effective plan.

warning.title: Your body needs recovery.
warning.message: You've been training hard lately — make sure to recover well before your next intense session.

warning.title: You haven't run in {days} days
warning.message: A short break happens! Your plan can be adjusted for a gentle return.

critical.title: {days}-day break — ready to come back?
critical.message: After a long period without training, it's recommended to ease back in to avoid injury.

recalibration.autoToggleDesc: Adjusts paces and load at the end of each week

postPlan.title: Plan complete — what's next?
postPlan.subtitle: You reached your goal. Choose your next step.

transition.title: Transition plan
transition.desc: 2 to 4 weeks of active post-race recovery at reduced volume.

maintenance.title: Maintenance plan
maintenance.desc: 4-week cycles to keep your fitness without a specific goal.

newGoal.title: New goal
newGoal.desc: Set a new race and generate a preparation plan.

vdot.label: VDOT
vdot.noData: No data available to estimate your VDOT.
vdot.description: VDOT is an aerobic performance index derived from VO₂max, developed by Jack Daniels. It reflects your ability to use oxygen during effort and determines your optimal training paces.
vdot.interpretation: The higher the VDOT, the better your endurance. A beginner runner is around 30–35, intermediate between 40–50, elite athletes above 60.

easy.name: Easy
easy.description: Easy conversational run. Builds aerobic base, improves running economy and supports active recovery. Should represent 70–80% of your total volume.

marathon.name: Marathon
marathon.description: Target marathon race pace. Stimulates metabolic efficiency at high aerobic intensity and trains the body to use fat as fuel.

threshold.name: Tempo
threshold.description: Lactate threshold pace — comfortably hard effort. Pushes back the anaerobic threshold, allowing you to sustain faster paces for longer.

interval.name: Intervals
interval.description: VO₂max pace — very intense effort, 3–5 min. Develops your maximum cardiovascular capacity. Demanding sessions, use sparingly.

repetition.name: Repetitions
repetition.description: Short sprint pace — maximal effort, 30–90 s. Improves running economy, stride mechanics and neuromuscular coordination.

ctl.label: CTL
ctl.name: Fitness
ctl.description: Chronic Training Load — 42-day exponentially weighted average. Represents your accumulated long-term fitness level.
ctl.interpretation: Ideally between 40 and 80 for a regular runner. Below 20: low fitness. Above 100: very high load (advanced athletes).

atl.label: ATL
atl.name: Fatigue
atl.description: Acute Training Load — 7-day exponentially weighted average. Reflects recently accumulated fatigue.
atl.interpretation: High ATL relative to CTL indicates overtraining risk. It rises quickly during heavy weeks and drops fast with rest.

tsb.label: TSB
tsb.name: Freshness
tsb.description: Training Stress Balance — difference CTL − ATL. Measures your current form and recovery state.
tsb.interpretation: Between −10 and +5: optimal training state. Above +10: well rested (peak form or detraining). Below −20: risk of excessive fatigue.

acwr.label: ACWR
acwr.name: Load ratio
acwr.description: Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio (ATL ÷ CTL). Injury risk indicator linked to too-rapid load increases.
acwr.interpretation: The 'green zone' is between 0.8 and 1.3. Below: underload (detraining). Above 1.5: red zone, significantly increased injury risk.

history.title: Plan History
history.emptyDescription: Your completed or abandoned plans will appear here.
